title: Silver polishing paste
subtitle: A gentle bicarbonate paste for tarnished silver
excerpt: A two-ingredient paste made with bicarbonate of soda and water that removes tarnish from silver cutlery, jewellery, and decorative items. Apply with a soft cloth, rinse, and dry immediately. Takes about 5 minutes to mix and use.
sourceNotes: Bicarbonate of soda (sodium bicarbonate) as a mild abrasive for silver tarnish removal: documented in multiple home-care references and supported by the chemistry of silver sulphide (tarnish) abrasion. The aluminium foil electrochemical method is a related but separate technique. Cream of tartar as an accelerant: traditional household recipe used to speed tarnish removal.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 small jar (~100 g, sufficient for multiple polishing sessions)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Make small batches as the paste dries out over time.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make a small batch and seal in a jar. Add a few drops of water and stir if the paste dries between uses.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Store in a sealed jar. The paste dries out over time as the water evaporates; add a few drops of water and stir to restore. Use within 3 months.

glossaryTerms.definition: A thin layer of silver sulphide that forms on the surface of silver when it reacts with sulphur compounds in the air. It appears as a dull grey, brown, or black discolouration. Bicarbonate of soda's mild abrasive action removes the sulphide layer without scratching the silver beneath.

content.text: forms when silver reacts with sulphur compounds in air, food, and household products. Bicarbonate of soda is a mild abrasive softer than silver; it removes the sulphide layer without scratching. Cream of tartar boosts the cleaning action without adding abrasion. The paste takes 2 minutes to make. It works on silver cutlery, jewellery, and decorative pieces. It is not suitable for very thin silver plate, items with non-metallic inlays or stones, or pieces with a deliberately applied antique patina.

items.prepNote: Accelerates tarnish removal; potassium hydrogen tartrate
Enough to form a thick paste; add gradually

content.text: Combine the bicarbonate of soda and cream of tartar in a small bowl. Add the distilled water a teaspoon at a time, stirring between each addition, until the mixture forms a smooth paste with the consistency of toothpaste. It should spread easily but not be runny.

Apply a small amount of paste to a soft, damp cloth or a soft toothbrush. Work the paste over the tarnished silver in gentle circular motions; the black or grey tarnish will transfer to the cloth. For heavy tarnish, leave the paste on the surface for 1 to 2 minutes before rubbing. Rinse the silver thoroughly under warm running water and dry immediately with a clean, soft cloth. Silver left damp will re-tarnish faster.

Use on solid silver, sterling silver, silver plate with thick plating, and silver jewellery with smooth stone settings. Do not use on pieces with a deliberately darkened patina, glued stone settings, gilt or gold-washed silver, very thin silver plate, or lacquered items.
Store in a sealed jar. As the paste dries out between uses, add a few drops of water and stir to restore the consistency. Use within 3 months.
